title: ‘They’d call us animals’ – World Cup final security slammed amid two-hour queues and ‘total chaos’
description: The security outside of the New York New Jersey Stadium ahead of the 2026 World Cup final has been criticized. Supporters attending the match have faced long queues just to enter the stadium before Spain play Argentina at 3pm EDT.
fullText:
The security outside of the New York New Jersey Stadium ahead of the 2026 World Cup final has been criticized.

Supporters attending the match have faced long queues just to enter the stadium before Spain play Argentina at 3pm EDT.

The United States Secret Service are said to have taken over security measures for the game because US president Donald Trump will be attending a match for the first time during this summer’s tournament.

Just a few hours before kick-off, fans and journalists launched fierce criticisms of the operation.

Mariana Spinelli, who works for leading Brazilian outlet Globo, took to social media platform X to write: “We’ve been in line for 2 hours for the x-ray to ENTER the stadium. Everything disorganized, a total chaos. If this were in South America, they’d call us animals, third-world country. Since it’s in the US nobody says anything. A nightmare of organization, pathetic.”

Spinelli is clearly of the belief that if such events were to happen in South America, there would be a much higher level of outrage.

Zach Lowy, the chief editor of Breaking The Lines, wrote: “The organization for this World Cup Final has been shambolic. Thousands who’ve been waiting for 3 hours, there’s a lack of security and officials and scanners, a lot of people who are cutting in line, and a lot of people who have gone around in circles without any progress.”

A further complication also arose when soccer fans were warned to attend the stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey up to four hours before the kick off time. Gates opened at 11am EDT.

A glitch in the FIFA security system to scan passes resulted in long queues forming.

There are believed to be two levels of security at the stadium, one of which is run by the Secret Service and the other organized by the Transportation Security Administration (TSA).

TSA usually oversee airport security. Fans and members of the media attending Sunday’s World Cup final having to pass through airport style scanners before being admitted into the venue.

title: When will the United States host the World Cup again?
description: Fans likely won't have to wait too long to see the World Cup return to the U.S.
fullText:
The United States had to wait 32 years between opportunities to host the men's World Cup. But after the final whistle sounds in Sunday's World Cup Final between Spain and Argentina at MetLife Stadium, U.S. fans shouldn't expect nearly as long of a gap between World Cups stateside.

FIFA's questionable manipulation of the hosting cycles will make that possible.

Basically, FIFA's process for choosing the World Cup hosts goes in a cycle between continental confederations. The 2030 World Cup wasn't just awarded to Spain and Portugal, but Morocco also joined the bid to use Africa's turn in the cycle with Europe. Then, FIFA announced that it would hold opening centennial matches in Argentina, Uruguay and Paraguay. This exhausted CONMEBOL's (South America) turn in the cycle.

While this maneuvering was an obvious effort to award the 2034 World Cup to Saudi Arabia ahead of its usual turn, it also made a quicker return to the U.S. a near certainty.

FIFA has yet to award the 2038 World Cup hosting rights, but fans should expect the tournament to return to the U.S., Mexico and Canada.

Only Oceania and North America would be eligible to host the 2038 World Cup under the current cycle rules. But with Australia competing in the Asian confederation, no viable host exists in Oceania. New Zealand doesn't have the stadium infrastructure.

And given the success of the 2026 World Cup, FIFA is absolutely going to look to cash in on another U.S. World Cup as soon as possible.

Countries eligible to host the 2038 FIFA World Cup 

(by u/-yolewpaniaq) pic.twitter.com/dOEAsoAHAN

— OnlMaps (@onlmaps) July 18, 2026

The 2031 Women's World Cup will also be in North America with Mexico, the United States, Costa Rica and Jamaica hosting the tournament.

This article originally appeared on For The Win: When will the United States host the World Cup again?

title: IShowSpeed kicks off World Cup closing ceremony in blaze of colour
description: Another star-studded performance at half-time will include Shakira, BTS, Madonna and Justin Bieber.
fullText:
The 2026 World Cup closing ceremony has kicked off with a performance from streamer IShowSpeed, who took to the field alongside drummers and dancers in vibrant tracksuits.

Rapper and singer Post Malone followed, performing his hit song Wow.

More than 80,000 fans, celebrities and foreign dignitaries are expected to pack New York New Jersey Stadium for Sunday's final between Spain and Argentina.

The star-studded pre-match entertainment is also expected to bring in actor Tom Cruise alongside singers Robbie Williams, Laura Pausini and Nicole Scherzinger.

Watch closing ceremony and build-up before Spain face Argentina in World Cup final

Italian singer Pausini told the BBC's Carl Nasman that she's "very excited" and "proud" to be performing alongside Williams and Scherzinger at the closing ceremony.

"When Robbie [Williams] invited me to sing with him, I felt goosebumps everywhere because Robbie's one of my favorite singer, songwriter, and performer," Pausini told the BBC.

The celebrity line-up will continue at half-time with performances by Madonna, Justin Bieber, Shakira and BTS.

Madonna, the best-selling female music artist of all time, is set to perform after the launch of her new album, Confessions II, which debuted at number one on the Billboard 200 chart in July.

Bieber also returned to the spotlight earlier this year, with his biggest live performance in four years at the Coachella music festival after cancelling his Justice world tour because of health issues.

Colombian pop star Shakira and Nigerian musician Burna Boy will reunite on stage after performing together at the tournament's opening ceremony in Mexico City last month.

Their collaboration, Dai Dai, has been a global chart hit and has been a fixture in stadiums throughout the tournament.

K-Pop group BTS will also perform after making their comeback from a three-year music hiatus to complete their mandatory military service in South Korea. The band is currently in the midst of an 85-date world tour.

The half-time break is expected to last 20-25 minutes, with Fifa saying the Super-Bowl-style performance will take 11 minutes.

It has been curated by Coldplay's Chris Martin and will raise money for the Fifa Global Citizen Education Fund, an initiative working to raise $100m (£73m) for children worldwide.

title: Donald Trump goes off-script on 'bagpipes' tangent in World Cup Final interview
description: President Donald Trump was interviewed ahead of the FIFA World Cup Final between Argentina and Spain, but the 80-year-old went off-script and began rambling about Scotland and bagpipes during the conversation
fullText:
President Donald Trump went off on a tangent during a pre-match interview ahead of the FIFA World Cup Final.

On July 19, Argentina faces Spain in the Final of the World Cup. The two nations battle for the world championship title at MetLife Stadium, branded as New York/New Jersey Stadium by the tournament, in East Rutherford, New Jersey.

Prior to the match on Fox Sports Live, President Donald Trump, 80, was questioned about how the United States Men's National Team had captured the world's attention, via The Mirror US.

Donald Trump sparks outrage with Harry Kane World Cup comments

Kylian Mbappe drops World Cup truth bomb after absurd third-place loss to England

Instead, Trump veered off-topic and began talking about Scotland — this following his boasts about pressuring FIFA chief Gianni Infantino over USMNT striker Folarin Balogun's red card, which triggered widespread controversy.

TV personality Jenny Taft, 38, posed the question: "It has been so special to see fans all over the world fall in love with our country. How proud does that make you?"

Trump responded: "Well, I watched, and it started with Scotland, and all of a sudden they're doing things that nobody has ever seen with the bagpipes. And it was beautiful.

"And it just went to every country. And we're the hottest country in the world right now," he concluded.

Trump's previous World Cup comments also drew significant backlash. England head coach Thomas Tuchel delivered a sharp rebuke after the President took aim at the team following their World Cup semifinal defeat to Argentina, during which Trump specifically criticized the tactical deployment of captain Harry Kane.

To listen to the latest episode of 'All Out Soccer' - CLICK HERE

"Harry, who's been fantastic, I think they perhaps made a mistake when they made him a defensive player. What do I know about soccer?" Trump initially said.

"They took the lead, and they took their best player and then put him on defense. What do I know about coaching? But that was a little unusual," he closed.

Tuchel was questioned about Trump's remarks ahead of England's third-place match against France on July 18. "Do you use Donald Trump as your witness for the case?" the ex-Chelsea boss responded to the reporter.

England then produced their finest performance of the tournament. England defeated France 6-4 in a thriller.

Following the victory over the French, Tuchel dismissed Trump's criticism and declared, "This game will help us for sure – even if you can never fully celebrate a bronze medal.

"It's the first World Cup medal for 60 years, first at a World Cup on foreign soil, and I hope the players can be proud of that at some point.

"We set ourselves the highest dream to chase, and we were very, very ambitious with our dream to make the final in the World Cup. So it's very, very painful if you miss out; the pain will stay for a while," he finished.

title: DeChambeau is not a cheater, Scheffler says as Open slips away
description: World number one Scottie Scheffler has defended Bryson DeChambeau over the rules controversy that dominated the Open, insisting his fellow US star is “not a cheater. ” DeChambeau was at the centre of attention at Royal Birkdale after being penalised two strokes following his second round for improving the line of his swing by trampling on long grass close to his ball.
fullText:
World number one Scottie Scheffler has defended Bryson DeChambeau over the rules controversy that dominated the Open, insisting his fellow US star is “not a cheater.”

DeChambeau was at the centre of attention at Royal Birkdale after being penalised two strokes following his second round for improving the line of his swing by trampling on long grass close to his ball.

The incident sparked a furore, with DeChambeau seen remonstrating with officials after being taken back to the scene of the incident on the fifth hole to review the matter.

Rumours circulated he had threatened to quit the tournament in protest and some of his fellow professionals were irritated that the affair delayed the publication of tee times for Saturday’s play.

World number two Rory McIlroy criticized DeChambeau’s “performative” behaviour, which included an after-dark session on the range in front of watching media.

However, tournament organizer the R&A accepted DeChambeau’s actions had been unintentional and Scheffler felt that point should be stressed.

Scheffler said on Sunday: “All I’ll say is Bryson, I’ve known him for a long time and he’s a lot of things, but he’s definitely not a cheater.

“I’ve never seen him cheat once, whether we were having a putting contest on the putting green or chipping contest, or anytime we played amateur golf, I never saw anything suspicious.

“When you’re watching him there from that camera angle, maybe he was a touch careless around his ball, but I don’t think he did anything intentionally.”

Scheffler believes DeChambeau’s high profile means his every move comes under greater scrutiny, although he also feels the game needs to find a fairer balance when enforcing rules.

He said: “I think maybe we need to be a little bit more strict in the way that we are policing guys, but it’s a little bit of a balance because you’ve got certain guys like Bryson who every shot he hits is going to be shown on TV.

“When you have camera angles that are far away, things can look different than they do when you’re right there.

“I feel like at times, sometimes you see players maybe push it a little bit further than they should.

“I think we need to kind of get back to the traditions of the game and maybe we do need to police things a little bit more stricter in the game.”

Scheffler’s defence of the Claret Jug ended in a tie for fourth, three shots behind winner Ryan Fox, despite a closing 67.

He said: “Today was a great effort. I felt like I was doing the right things out there, I gave myself a lot of looks, but I guess it just wasn’t my week.”

title: Post Malone Debuts New Song ‘Chrome Heartbreaker’ at World Cup Final Pregame Ceremony
description: Jennifer Hudson delivered the United States national anthem and IShowSpeed performed "Champions" before Spain and Argentina match at New York New Jersey Stadium
fullText:
Post Malone headlined the 2026 FIFA World Cup Final opening ceremony at the “New York New Jersey” Stadium, performing a genre-spanning set for the massive crowd that gathered 90 minutes before kickoff.

Malone opened with his performance with “Chrome Heartbreaker,” a new song he’d been teasing the past week, which was followed by his hits “Wow” and “Sunflower,” the latter featuring Swae Lee:

Post Malone to Perform at the World Cup Closing Ceremony

IShowSpeed Talks Travel Essentials, Surviving 40,000 Steps a Day, and Why He 'Almost Cried' Watching Ronaldo Score

Alicia Keys, Bruce Springsteen, Jennifer Hudson, and More to Attend Clive Davis Funeral

Post Malone performando “Chrome Heartbreaker” na cerimônia de encerramento da Copa do Mundo Fifa 2026. pic.twitter.com/96KhO38OKN

— Portal Post Malone 🍻 | Fansite (@PortalPostBR) July 19, 2026

Malone’s set lived up to the description FIFA President Gianni Infantino gave earlier in the week when the announcement was made. “At a time when sport, culture, and global attention converge, Post Malone will deliver a performance designed to celebrate the tournament’s journey and ignite the atmosphere before the world’s attention turns to the two finalists,” Infantino previously said in a statement.

Post Malone and Swae Lee sing “Sunflower” at the 2026 World Cup opening ceremony 🌻

🎥 @TelemundoSports | #FIFAWorldCuppic.twitter.com/2Gbdw3pF4v

— The Athletic (@TheAthletic) July 19, 2026

While Malone headlined the show, a few additional heavy hitters joined the pregame festivities, including Tom Cruise, who made an appearance ahead of the release of his latest movie, Digger, set for fall 2026. Jennifer Hudson was also on hand for her rousing rendition of the United States national anthem, while YouTube star IShowSpeed performed his song “Champions”:

🎤 @ishowspeedsui performs his song "Champions" during the 2026 FIFA World Cup Closing Ceremony pic.twitter.com/cmNqWIUDLE

— FOX Sports (@FOXSports) July 19, 2026

More superstars are set to perform during the halftime show, with Justin Bieber headlining alongside co-headliners Madonna, Shakira, and BTS, curating a once-in-a-lifetime experience for World Cup final attendees. The performances have been stacked from the start of the month-long tournament across its three host territories: the U.S., Canada, and Mexico.

Shakira and Burna Boy launched the World Cup in Mexico City with a performance of “Dai Dai.” Label mates Future and Tyla, alongside Katy Perry, performed during the World Cup opener in the U.S. at SoFi Stadium in Los Angeles, while Canada’s opening ceremony featured Michael Bublé, Alessia Cara, and Eliana. Ellie Goulding appeared at the quarterfinals, where her home country of England defeated Norway, before ultimately being eliminated after a semifinal loss to Argentina.

Malone, who is in the midst of his Big Ass Stadium Tour with Jelly Roll, closed out what has been a star-studded run of World Cup musical performances. As the 2026 World Cup story nears its end and fans wait to see whether Spain or Argentina will be crowned champion, the next FIFA World Cup has been set for 2030. It will mark the tournament’s 100th anniversary, with Morocco, Portugal, and Spain confirmed as hosts.

title: World Cup final half-time show: Who is performing, how long will it take and how can I watch it?
description: The finale will feature a half-time show with some of the biggest names in music, including Madonna, Shakira and BTS
fullText:
The World Cup 2026 final is drawing closer with Spain booking their place in Sunday’s match in New Jersey after a fine display to beat France 2-0.

England battle Argentina in tonight’s other semi-final in Atlanta to decide the other finalist.

But Fifa President Gianni Infantino has modernised the tournament with influence from its co-hosts United States and the way some of its biggest sporting events operate, including the Super Bowl in the NFL.

World Cup final live: Spain v Argentina team news and half-time show updates

This will mean a half-time show appealing to more forms of culture and new fans.

Here’s everything you need to know about the Fifa World Cup 2026 final half-time show:

The half-time show will be on Sunday, 19 July 2026 at New York New Jersey Stadium, USA, the location for the final match, with kick-off set at 8pm BST (3pm ET), the half-time show is due to start from 8:50pm BST (3:50pm ET), with stoppage time and additional time to set up the stage and infrastructure perhaps leading to a start time after 9pm BST (4pm ET).

Sources tell the Independent that the show will be around 20 minutes. There is the prospect of the usual 15-minute half-time break, followed by the planned 11-minute show, with Infantino emphasising the figure is inspired by 11 players on the pitch for each team.

Due to the laws of the game, governed by Ifab (the International Football Association Board), it is understood half-time breaks should “not exceed15 minutes,” but Sunday is due to break that law.

There is a precedent set from last year’s Club World Cup final between eventual winners Chelsea and Paris Saint-Germain, which was also organised by Fifa and held in the same venue in New Jersey. That show lasted 24 minutes, with Coldplay, J Balvin, Doja Cat, Tems and Emmanuel Kelly featuring.

The game is on both BBC and ITV in the UK, with ITV revealing that it will broadcast the entire World Cup half-time show at the New York New Jersey Stadium, as well as match analysis.

The BBC will confirm its plans in the coming hours. Alternatively you can follow The Independent’s coverage in our live blog, which will be packed with updates from the show and reaction from social media and our reporters in the stadium.

The 11-minute Super Bowl-style show will be co-headlined by Madonna, Shakira and K-pop boy band BTS.

Justin Bieber was added to the bill, while Burna Boy, Gustavo Dudamel and the PS22 Chorus featuring Coldplay also involved, and Chris Martin has curated the show.

The closing ceremony starts at 18:30 BST (1:30pm ET), with the show including Tom Cruise, Laura Pausini, Nicole Scherzinger, Robbie Williams and IShowSpeed. And Jennifer Hudson is to sing the United States' national anthem.

title: Donald Trump predicts the winner of Spain vs Argentina FIFA World Cup final
description: Spain vs Argentina will be the 23rd edition of the FIFA World Cup final at the MetLife Stadium on Sunday evening as the best tournament on earth comes to an end for another four years. Of the 48 final teams, just two remain as the champions of Europe, Spain, face defending world champions Argentina.
fullText:
Spain vs Argentina will be the 23rd edition of the FIFA World Cup final at the MetLife Stadium on Sunday evening as the best tournament on earth comes to an end for another four years.

Of the 48 final teams, just two remain as the champions of Europe, Spain, face defending world champions Argentina.

Soccer fans across the globe are divided on who will be crowned champions ahead of kick-off and naturally, US President Donald Trump was asked for his prediction.

Jenny Taft of Fox Sports caught up with Mr Trump hours before kick-off as the president prepared to take his seat at the biggest sporting occasion on the planet.

He was asked: “I’ve got to ask, Argentina-Spain, give us your prediction.”

He said: “Well, I knew you were going to ask, so I thought about it very hard. I hate to get involved even though politically it doesn’t matter too much.

“The head of Argentina is a friend of mine who has done a terrific job, but I would say it’s hard to bet against Messi. I watched that pass (vs England) I think you’d call it a pass. It was exactly perfect.

“It takes some talent to hit the ball off the head coming at that speed, but that was perfect if you had a strike zone, that would be perfect. Just before that, the way he got away from the defender, he was defended very well; all of a sudden, he’s standing by himself, so it’s hard to bet against him.

“I won’t pick sides but I think it’s very hard to bet against Messi; he’s great.”

title: Evans sixth in Estonia but extends WRC lead
description: Welshman Elfyn Evans extends his lead in his bid for a first World Rally Championship title despite finishing sixth at Delfi Rally Estonia.
fullText:
Elfyn Evans extended his lead in his bid for a first World Rally Championship (WRC) title despite finishing sixth at Delfi Rally Estonia.

Sami Pajari's maiden win alongside co-driver and Finnish compatriot Marko Salminen helped him rise to third in the rankings.

Welshman Evans and partner Scott Martin finished just over two minutes behind Pajari on Sunday after the 18 stages that began on Friday.

Japan hope Takamoto Katsuta held on to second spot in the title standings after a 21st-place finish, but Evans gained eight points to establish a 15-point lead over his nearest challenger.

Sweden's Oliver Sojberg and British co-driver Elliott Edmondson took second in Estonia with Adran Formaux (France) third, Thierry Neuville (Belgium) fourth and Sebastien Ogier (France) fifth.

Motorsport Ireland's Jon Armstrong was ninth, but after taking a best-ever fourth in Greece in the last round, fellow countryman Josh McErlean ended the Estonia event 43rd.

The teams will now prepare for the remaining five rallies, starting in Finland from 30 July-2 August, that will decide the 2026 winner.

title: Shriever wins historic fourth BMX world title
description: Beth Shriever becomes the first BMX racer to win four elite world titles as extreme weather curtails the UCI BMX Racing World Championships in Brisbane.
fullText:
Britain's Beth Shriever has become the first BMX racer to win four elite world titles as extreme weather curtailed the UCI BMX Racing World Championships in Brisbane.

After persistent strong winds delayed the start of the race in the Australian city, the UCI was forced to abandon the event and take results from the first round, held the previous day.

Shriever, 27, set the fastest lap time in the women's event, which meant she was declared the champion for an unprecedented fourth time.

In the men's event, fellow Briton Ross Cullen won silver after setting the second-fastest lap, behind Australia's Jesse Asmus.

Shriever's victory adds to her world titles in 2021, 2023 and 2025, as well her Olympic gold at Tokyo 2020.

"I've dreamed about doing this, but obviously not in this way. I know it's a crazy, crazy day and I think all of us feel like this is a bit of a dream right now," said Shriever.

"But, I mean, I laid down a solid lap yesterday, so everything happens for a reason. I couldn't believe how quick yesterday was so, yeah, we'll take it.

"I'm still happy, obviously we wanted to do it properly today but we can't control the weather and it was too dangerous out there today."

According to UCI regulations, if a BMX Racing World Championships is cancelled, "the last completed round will be declared the final results".

The decision to abandon the race was taken with the support of the UCI Athletes' Commission representative on site, the National Federation Team Managers, and the Local Organising Committee.

In the women's junior event, Freia Challis pipped fellow Briton Elsa Rendall Todd to win gold.

title: World Cup error will make Lammens stronger - Heaton
description: Tom Heaton is backing Manchester United team-mate Senne Lammens to emerge as a stronger character from his World Cup disappointment.
fullText:
Tom Heaton is backing Manchester United team-mate Senne Lammens to emerge as a stronger character from his World Cup disappointment.

Lammens had been back-up to Thibaut Courtois throughout the tournament until the veteran Belgium goalkeeper was injured 19 minutes from the end of the quarter-final against Spain.

The 24-year-old had barely touched the ball before he spilled Pau Cubarsi's shot late in the game, which allowed Arsenal's Mikel Merino to steer home and give Spain a 2-1 victory.

Lammens was distraught at the final whistle and Heaton has spoken to the youngster, who was outstanding at United last season after arriving as a relative unknown from Royal Antwerp in preference to Argentina's Emi Martinez, for £18.1m.

"Yes, I spoke to him," said Heaton after Manchester United's opening pre-season defeat by Wrexham in Helsinki.

"It was obviously a challenging moment for him, but that's the life of a goalkeeper.

"As soon as you put the gloves on and go between the sticks, you have to be ready to deal with that.

"He'll be stronger for it. I think his personality is fantastic for a goalkeeper, so no issues there. As I say, I honestly think it can help him in the long run."

Lammens is now on a three-week break before returning to pre-season training.

It means he should return in the first week of August and, in theory, be available for the Gothenburg encounter with Paris St-Germain on 8 August.

Despite the criticism Lammens received in Belgium for his error, at United, confidence in the youngster remains strong.

Karl Darlow, 35, was signed as back-up to Lammens on 14 July and while Heaton, 40, still views himself as a player after signing a one-year deal in the summer, his last competitive senior appearance was in February 2023.

"I highly doubt Senne will be going into it with fear or worrying about what may happen," said Heaton.

"That can happen at any point. Literally every time you cross the foot over the earth. If that was your mindset, I'd be inclined to say it's more likely to happen.

"Goalkeeper is a position where if something goes wrong, it's going to end in a goal. Goals can change games and cause you to lose. You have to be realistic about that.

"It is tough, but Senne is very strong mentally. I saw that from him as a young goalkeeper coming into Manchester United and dealing with it unbelievably well."

title: How the World Cup has affected your Premier League team
description: Once the trophy is lifted by either Spain or Argentina in New Jersey on Sunday night, attention will shift from the 2026 World Cup to the upcoming domestic season.
fullText:
Once the World Cup is lifted by either Argentina or Spain in New Jersey on Sunday night, attention will quickly shift to the new domestic season.

The Premier League was well represented in North America, with all 20 of the top-flight teams for 2026-27 having at least one player involved.

From injuries to transfers, here is how the World Cup has impacted your Premier League team.

When the World Cup finishes, the football continues on BBC Sport

Arsenal may be the Premier League team affected most by the World Cup.

The Gunners face an anxious wait over the fitness of William Saliba, who had been managing a back issue before his tournament was ended by the same problem.

The centre-back has not yet been assessed by the club's medical team, but if he was sidelined, it would be a big loss - especially as his defensive partnership with Gabriel is so crucial to how the team play.

The other two question marks will be over the fitness of Declan Rice and Bukayo Saka, who helped England finish third while managing their own fitness issues.

It does not look like they will need a longer lay-off once the tournament is over, but they will be monitored closely by Arsenal before the new season.

Leandro Trossard enjoyed a fine tournament with Belgium, starting all six games and scoring twice as they reached the quarter-finals, before the forward joined Turkish club Besiktas for £15m.

Gabriel Martinelli, who showed flashes of his quality playing for Brazil, is another player the Gunners could be open to selling this summer.

Carlo Ancelotti played the winger in a central role during the tournament, and that may be an extra attraction for potential buyers.

Aston Villa's transfer plans were thrown a curveball by an injury to Amadou Onana.

The midfielder ruptured the anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) in his right knee during Belgium's last-16 win over the USA which will rule him out until next year.

They were not planning significant changes in midfield, although an extra body would have been useful, so moved for Johan Manzambi and Joao Gomes - especially with Youri Tielemans joining Manchester United.

Manzambi - who has completed a club record move for more than £50m from Freiburg - excelled for Switzerland, scoring three goals, and will form part of Villa's new-look squad as Unai Emery tries to refresh it amid potential further sales.

Chelsea have agreed a £117m deal to sign England midfielder Morgan Rogers, while France's Lucas Digne is due to join Paris St-Germain, but goalkeeper Emi Martinez is likely to stay - and might return with another World Cup winners' medal as Argentina defend their title against Spain on Sunday.

Bournemouth fans will have been encouraged by the performances of Ben Gannon-Doak at the World Cup, despite Scotland's brief stay in the tournament. The winger played in all three group matches, and particularly impressed in their sole win over Haiti.

The Cherries had six players at the World Cup, including New Zealand back-up goalkeeper Alex Paulsen who has joined Motherwell on loan for the coming season.

Starring among them, as well as Gannon-Doak, was winger Rayan. The 19-year-old started all Brazil's games bar their opener and claimed an assist in the 3-0 win over Scotland to seal their path to the knockout stage.

It further raises Rayan's stock following his January move to Bournemouth, and superb performances in the second half of last season.

This could be bad news for Bournemouth though, with Rayan heavily linked with Liverpool, managed by former Cherries boss Andoni Iraola.

For a couple of Brentford players, the World Cup could not have gone much worse.

Striker Igor Thiago came into the tournament on the back of a stellar scoring season for the Bees and tipped as Brazil's lead attacker in North America. However, after a lacklustre performance in the opener against Morocco he was dropped by manager Carlo Ancelotti and did not appear again.

It remains to be seen what impact this has on Thiago's confidence heading into the new season.

Meanwhile, midfielder Jordan Henderson made one substitute appearance for England, but his most memorable contribution was falling over an advertising hoarding during the celebrations following the win over Mexico and requiring surgery on a broken wrist.

Henderson, however, stayed with the England squad and should be fit for the start of Brentford's season, though he may have to initially play in a cast.

Brighton went into the World Cup with eight representatives and emerged with eight - although the personnel has changed.

Dutch centre-back Jan Paul van Hecke has moved to Tottenham from Brighton, with Croatia defender Luka Vuskovic going the other way.

Vuskovic endured a difficult World Cup, subbed off after 66 minutes of Croatia's opening loss to England, then not appearing in their three subsequent games.

Indeed, the only one of Brighton's eight representatives to make it past the last 16 was Belgium left-back Maxim de Cuyper who played in all six of their matches, starting five.

This could be useful for Brighton, who will hope key players like Bart Verbruggen, Pascal Gross and Yasin Ayari will therefore not be too tired after the World Cup and fresh for the start of the Premier League season.

Chelsea had 11 players involved at the World Cup, but only seven played in the tournament.

Key players Cole Palmer, Levi Colwill and Joao Pedro were left out of their respective squads and have already returned for pre-season training.

The greatest concern has been the fitness of captain Reece James, who played into the latter stages of the tournament after helping England reach the third-place play-off, but suffered hamstring issues.

Enzo Fernandez and Malo Gusto also reached the latter stages of the competition and are set to return late for pre-season, along with expected new signing Valentin Barco.

England midfielder Morgan Rogers is set to arrive at Chelsea from Aston Villa for that club record £117m deal, though international team-mate Trevoh Chalobah is expected to leave this summer.

Much like Cristiano Ronaldo after Portugal knocked England out of the European Championship in 2006, Fernandez may find his antics in and around Argentina's victory over England will make him a target for opposition fans next season should he stay with Chelsea.

New Coventry defender Aurele Amenda suffered World Cup frustration. The centre-back, who has joined from Frankfurt, was an unused substitute in Switzerland's run to the quarter-finals.

Striker Haji Wright played against Australia and Belgium as co-hosts USA bowed out to the Red Devils in the last 16.

The 28-year-old will be one of Coventry's main threats as they return to the Premier League for the first time in 25 years, having scored 20 goals to help them to the Championship title last season.

Fellow forward Brandon Thomas-Asante, who scored 13 goals for the Sky Blues last season, also featured in two of Ghana's World Cup games as they exited the competition in the last 32.

Crystal Palace were one of the most represented Premier League clubs at the World Cup, with 10 players involved.

They received a potential blow, however, when Spain winger Yeremy Pino sustained a shoulder injury during the group stage, but he may be called upon for the World Cup final.

Elsewhere, goalscoring midfielder Daichi Kamada and attacking right-back Daniel Munoz impressed for Japan and Colombia respectively. New Palace manager Pierre Sage will hope the club rebuffs any potential suitors.

Other key players like goalkeeper Dean Henderson and striker Jean-Philippe Mateta have been peripheral figures for England and France, so should be raring to go when they return for pre-season training.

Iliman Ndiaye is one of Everton's biggest assets and was at the centre of transfer talk before the World Cup.

He made an impact at the tournament with a spectacular strike after coming on as a substitute in Senegal's 5-0 win against Iraq. They reached the last 32 before losing 3-2 to Belgium.

It remains to be seen whether the World Cup reignites speculation, but Everton will hope it stays quiet as Ndiaye is arguably their most creative player.

Idrissa Gueye, Ndiaye's Senegal team-mate, had his Everton deal expire at the start of July, so is effectively a free agent.

All talks on his future were shelved until the end of the World Cup, so a decision on the 36-year-old's next move will be made soon.

Nathan Patterson played for Scotland at right-back, but he has failed to convince manager David Moyes that he should be a regular starter at Everton.

The Toffees are actively pursuing targets in that position, so Patterson could yet be on the move before the start of the season.

It was a World Cup to remember for four out of the six Fulham players involved.

Sander Berge and Oscar Bobb were part of the brilliant Norway side beaten by England in the quarter-finals, while Belgium's Timothy Castagne and Morocco's Issa Diop also fell at that stage.

While Bobb was not often a starter for Norway, his second-half appearances allowed him to showcase his talents and ability to move the ball in the final third.

But for Fulham fans, it was a chance to catch a glimpse of defender Luc de Fougerolles.

The 20-year-old has been at Craven Cottage since 2023 but is yet to make an appearance after spending last season on loan at Belgium Pro League side Dender EH.

Perhaps new manager Alvaro Arbeloa will be pleased with what he saw.

Newly-promoted Hull watched two players leave for the World Cup and welcomed home one.

Croatia goalkeeper Ivor Pandur made the switch from the Tigers to Rangers for a fee of £6m at the end of June.

But moving the other way from Ibrox was Jack Butland to fill the goalkeeping void.

Hull fans did have something to cheer as Liam Millar progressed to the last 16 of the World Cup in a historic run for co-hosts Canada.

Hull boss Sergej Jakirovic can look forward to having an almost completely fresh squad ready to tackle pre-season before the Premier League starts in August.

Like fellow newly promoted Hull, Ipswich only had two players make their way to America, Canada and Mexico for the World Cup.

Striker George Hirst was named in the first Scotland squad to reach the World Cup in 26 years, while Ali Al-Hamadi was there with Iraq.

Hirst failed to make it off the bench for Scotland in a campaign that ended at the group stage, as Al-Hamadi appeared three times for winless Iraq.

For new Ipswich manager Gary O'Neil, it presents a chance to put out his pre-season ideas to an almost full squad - including new signing Emersonn.

The striker is the first Brazilian to sign for the Tractor Boys but, despite playing for his country at youth level, he is yet to win a senior cap.

Leeds fans could only keep an eye on four of their players at the World Cup - with new signing Harry Wilson's Wales failing to qualify.

Left-back Gabriel Gudmundsson was the Leeds player who appeared the most times in the tournament, featuring in all four of Sweden's matches.

Midfielder Ao Tanaka was left heartbroken by Japan's stoppage-time loss to Brazil in the last 32. He made three appearances for his nation - who many thought were dark horses for the tournament.

Despite coming within a whisker of the semi-finals with Switzerland, Noah Okafor will return to Elland Road fresh, with just one World Cup appearance to his name.

Leeds signed defender Tarik Muharemovic for £34.1m from Serie A side Sassuolo in the summer. The 23-year-old made three appearances for Bosnia-Herzegovina in the World Cup, including in the last-32 defeat by co-hosts the United States.

With survival the aim for Daniel Farke's side last season, now it is all about progression.

No matter what happens on Sunday, Liverpool will have a World Cup winner with Alexis Mac Allister playing for Argentina and new signing Victor Munoz representing Spain.

Munoz is yet to play a minute at the tournament but Mac Allister has excelled for his country after a below-par season at Anfield, and his effective display against England will certainly have pleased Iraola.

Aside from that, Liverpool will take plenty of confidence from the other players they had in North America. Alexander Isak scored once and got three assists for Sweden while Florian Wirtz provided three assists for Germany.

Cody Gakpo scored three times for the Netherlands, with Virgil van Dijk and Ryan Gravenberch also playing for them.

Meanwhile, Alisson played every minute for Brazil before they were knocked out by Norway in the last 16.

In terms of signings, Liverpool have expressed interest in Yan Diomande, while Bradley Barcola is also on their shortlist. Both impressed for Ivory Coast and France respectively.

Manchester City midfielder Rodri has played a starring role in captaining Spain to Sunday's World Cup final.

The 29-year-old's long-term future at Etihad Stadium is uncertain as he enters the final year of his contract, and reports suggest he is set to miss the start of the season to have surgery on an unspecified injury.

City hold a strong interest in Lille's Morocco midfielder Ayyoub Bouaddi after the teenager shone for the North Africans during the tournament, but it remains to be seen whether new manager Enzo Maresca makes a move for the 18-year-old who could cost up to £80m.

Club record signing Elliot Anderson enhanced his reputation in England's run to the semi-finals and striker Erling Haaland seamlessly transferred his club form to the world stage, but Rayan Cherki found minutes hard to come by with France.

Fifa has mandated three weeks off for players during the off-season, meaning the aforementioned City players will not be on the plane for the club's pre-season trip to the Far East unless joining later on.

The most concrete World Cup news for Manchester United was Manuel Ugarte's knee injury.

Ugarte is going to be out until 2027 after having surgery to repair cruciate ligament damage. Given the Uruguay midfielder was likely to be sold this summer, it has added an extra layer of complication around United's recruitment plans.

The 21-year-old shrugged off the disappointment at being continually overlooked by Ruben Amorim to deliver excellent performances over the second half of last season, which suggests he can bounce back from not playing for England at the World Cup.

However, Michael Carrick, who experienced the same thing under Fabio Capello in 2010, might need to put an arm around his shoulder.

It will also be interesting to see whether there is any fallout from Lisandro Martinez being one of the Argentina players to hold the 'Las Malvinas son Argentinas' banner up after the semi-final win against England.

Anthony Elanga and Yoane Wissa's performances for Sweden and DR Congo respectively will have offered Newcastle United a little encouragement.

The pair even scored as many goals combined (five) at the World Cup as they did during their challenging debut campaign at St James' Park.

Elsewhere, Newcastle captain Bruno Guimaraes may have missed a penalty in Brazil's last-16 defeat against Norway, but the midfielder still caught the eye by setting up four goals at the tournament.

It is easy to see why Newcastle do not want to lose their talisman after he intimated he wished to join Arsenal.

The sight of another midfielder, Johan Manzambi, impressing for Switzerland ultimately left Newcastle wondering what might have been after their top target opted to join Aston Villa instead.

Newcastle have targeted young players with a similar profile this summer, but head coach Eddie Howe will rely on experienced leaders like Dan Burn to help them along.

Burn, fresh from making his World Cup debut for England aged 34, still has a lot to offer.

Tottenham's Lucas Bergvall is a priority target for Nottingham Forest. They are looking to replace Elliot Anderson after his record move to Manchester City and Bergvall is their number one option.

The midfielder helped Sweden to the last 32 of the World Cup, where they lost 3-0 to France.

Winger Dan Ndoye was key in Switzerland's run to the quarter-finals and could flourish in Oliver Glasner's new team. He scored twice, including in Switzerland's quarter-final defeat by defending champions Argentina.

Austria reached the last 32 with Xaver Schlager featuring and the midfielder is expected to join Forest on a free transfer after leaving RB Leipzig.

Ibrahim Sangare helped Ivory Coast reach the last 32, where they lost to Norway, while Tyler Bindon and Chris Wood could not stop New Zealand exiting at the group stage.

Sunderland were among the Premier League clubs with the highest number of players participating at the World Cup and it is no surprise given they secured European football last season.

There were mixed results for the Black Cats contingent with captain Granit Xhaka narrowly missing out on a semi-final match against England.

Chemsdine Talbi was a bright spark for Morocco when used and Brian Brobbey proved as difficult to play against as ever when leading the line for the Netherlands.

Striker Wilson Isidor's journey ended at the group stage with Haiti but he will always have that stunning goal against Morocco to look back on.

To boost their already exciting squad, Sunderland have added experienced Belgium right-back Thomas Meunier to their ranks.

Tottenham Hotspur's main transfer business was done before the World Cup, with arrivals such as Sandro Tonali, Mateus Fernandes, Andrew Robertson, plus central defenders Marcos Senesi and Jan Paul van Hecke.

This has sparked talk of outgoings, which the tournament could have an impact on.

It is the third World Cup in succession that Spurs will be represented in the final, with Hugo Lloris playing for France in 2018 and 2022, while Argentina's Cristian Romero is making his second appearance.

The arrival of Senesi and Van Hecke has increased speculation about Spurs captain Romero's future, and his displays for Argentina will have further attracted the clubs who have been linked with him, including Atletico Madrid and Inter Milan.

Romero has the ultimate showcase when Argentina face Spain in the World Cup final. Another strong performance might bring a move closer, as well as increasing Spurs' bargaining position.

title: England seal series with record Lord's ODI total
description: England hit the highest one-day international total at Lord's to beat India by 27 runs and seal a thrilling 2-1 series victory.
fullText:
Third one-day international, Lord's

England 387-3 (50 overs): Duckett 141 (135), Bethell 91 (93), Root 74* (48); Krishna 2-69

India 360-7 (50 overs): Rohit 138 (110), Shubman 77 (84), Kohli 74 (60); Curran 4-75

England won by 27 runs, won three-match series 2-1

England hit the highest one-day international total at Lord's to beat India by 27 runs and seal a thrilling 2-1 series victory.

Opener Ben Duckett made the biggest men's ODI individual score at the Home of Cricket in striking 141 off 135 balls as the hosts posted 383-7, surpassing the previous mark of 334-4 by England against the same opponents in 1975.

Duckett shared an opening stand of 192 with Jacob Bethell, who fell for 91, before finally chipping a return catch to Prince Yadav in the 44th over after smacking 18 fours and a six.

Joe Root helped drive England to their formidable total, hitting an unbeaten 74 from 48 balls for his sixth consecutive score of 50 or 

Asked to reach what would have been the second-highest winning target in the format's history, India remained in the hunt deep in the game thanks to Rohit Sharma's superb 138.

The 39-year-old, who is rumoured to be retiring at the end of the series, was in sparkling form, hitting 17 fours and five sixes in his 111-ball knock.

He put on his own stand of 147 with Shubman Gill, who struck 77 before he was trapped lbw by Adil Rashid.

Rohit's dismissal in the 38th over, bowled round his legs by Bethell, was the decisive moment in the match, leaving Virat Kohli to attempt to take India to victory as the required run-rate spiralled.

Kohli saw the remainder of the India middle-order dismissed cheaply, and he himself was then caught in the covers off the bowling of Sam Curran for 74, effectively ending the tourists' challenge.

India's tail continued to hit boundaries, but their target was too great and they finished on 360-7.

Notifications, social media and more with BBC Sport

title: Trump presence means tight security for World Cup final
description: Supporters faced long queues to get into New York New Jersey Stadium amid heightened security for the 2026 World Cup final.
fullText:
Supporters faced long queues to get into New York New Jersey Stadium amid heightened security for the 2026 World Cup final.

Spain face Argentina at the 82,500-seater venue, with kick-off at 15:00 local time (20:00 BST).

There is increased security partly because United States president Donald Trump is attending the final - the only World Cup match he is watching in person.

As a result, the United States Secret Service took over security for the game - leading to long waits for supporters to get into the ground.

BBC Sport understands people were having to queue for about two hours.

But after the long queues, it is understood most fans had no issues scanning tickets and accessing the stadium.

There were two levels of security at the stadium, run by both the Secret Service and Transportation Security Administration (TSA).

The latter is usually responsible for security at airports in the United States, with fans and media having to go through airport security-style scanners.

One member of the media said they, having reached an entrance, were told to go to a different queue as there were no security dogs available to search bags at that gate.

BBC Sport has been told by World Cup volunteers at the ground that the number of people arriving early before the game was much higher than the previous seven matches at the stadium.

Gates opened for supporters at 11:00 local time - four hours before kick-off - with more gates being opened sporadically to ease congestion.

It is not thought there will be any delay to kick-off or any other disruption to the match.

When the World Cup finishes, the football continues on BBC Sport

title: Tom Heaton predicts Senne Lammens’ reaction to World Cup mistake
description: Manchester United veteran Tom Heaton has thrown his support behind Senne Lammens after the 23-year-old Belgian’s mistake at the World Cup, revealing how he will react to it next season.World Cup Hea...
fullText:
Manchester United veteran Tom Heaton has thrown his support behind Senne Lammens after the 23-year-old Belgian’s mistake at the World Cup, revealing how he will react to it next season.

After a superb debut season at Old Trafford following his £18.1 million move from Royal Antwerp last year, Lammens was selected by Rudi Garcia as part of his squad for this summer’s tournament in North America.

Real Madrid superstar Thibaut Courtois is the undisputed starter for Belgium, having made 115 appearances for his country.

Lammens was entrusted with the No. 2 role for the Red Devils, while Chelsea’s Mike Penders was the third choice. This constituted a remarkable rise for the United man, given he only made his first appearance for Belgium in November.

However, delight turned to disaster when Garcia summoned him from the bench in the quarter-final clash with Spain, after Courtois went down with a thigh injury in the 71st minute. Lammens entered the pitch with the game – his debut at a major tournament – poised at 1-1, with Charles De Ketelaere’s header having cancelled out Fabian Ruiz’s opener.

But as the clock ticked past the 88th minute, Lammens made a mess of a speculative shot from distance by Pau Cubarsí, spilling the ball into the path of the onrushing Mikel Merino, who converted to send Spain through to the semi-finals.

Naturally, Lammens was devastated at the final whistle, with Courtois making a beeline to console his compatriot, as did Spanish No. 1 Unai Simon to show the goalkeeping brotherhood extends beyond borders.

In a post-match interview, the Madrid shot stopper backed Lammens, stating: “You only get stronger from this. Eventually you cannot say much more to him or give him much more advice. He is a strong guy, strong personality. I’m sure he’ll be fine. You know, he will have some holidays and then regroup in Manchester and have a great season.”

This view is mirrored by his teammate at Old Trafford, with Heaton confirming he had spoken to Lammens after the quarter-final, but predicting he will “be stronger” for the experience, as tough as it is in the moment.

“It was obviously a challenging moment for him, but I think that’s the life of a goalkeeper. As soon as you put the gloves on and go between the sticks, you have to be ready to deal with that. He certainly is,” the 40-year-old stated.

“I actually think he’ll be stronger for it. His personality is fantastic for a goalkeeper, so no issues there. It’s obviously disappointing for him, but as I say, I do honestly think it can help him in the long run. It’s certainly tough but his personality is suited for it. He’s very strong mentally and he’ll be absolutely fine.”

When asked whether it was the type of high-profile mistake which can haunt a goalkeeper in the future, Heaton was confident it would not.

“That can happen at any point. Literally every time you cross the foot over the earth, so if that’s your mindset and that’s your outlook, then I’d be more inclined to say it’s more likely to happen.

“It’s accepting the fact that it can happen, but what you want to try and do and go and embrace it? I’ve certainly seen that from him as a young goalkeeper coming into Manchester United and dealing with that unbelievably well.

“I highly doubt he’ll be going into it with fear or worrying about what may happen, it’s more about what you want to go and achieve, whilst also accepting if it happens you can deal with it.”

A report relayed by The Peoples Person revealed Manchester United have planned to offer their full support to Lammens, imploring the team’s leadership group, which includes Heaton, to “reach out to him to show their support.”

But Heaton’s assured stance on the mental resilience of his younger teammate is promising – and is backed up by United legend Gary Pallister, who in an exclusive interview with our commander-in-chief, Red Billy, claimed Lammens is “strong enough to put that behind him”.

Feature image Orlando Ramirez via Getty Images

The Peoples Person has been one of the world’s leading Man United news sites for over a decade. bsky.social

title: England player ratings for entire World Cup: Wasted inclusion gets 2/10 but one star fetches 9/10
description: A ‘what could have been’ tournament brought England semi-final heartbreak versus Argentina, then a thrilling win over France for bronze medals
fullText:
Another World Cup is in the books, and you know what that means: four more years of hurt, minimum, in England’s bid to replicate the triumph of 1966.

The Three Lions arrived in North America with a 26-man squad and a German coach in Thomas Tuchel, who led the England side at a major tournament for the first time. And on the face of it, a semi-final run (just the fourth in England’s World Cup history) was an admirable effort, with no shame in losing to reigning champions Argentina.

Yet the late heartbreak in that semi-final was frustratingly familiar, with Tuchel under fire for his defensive approach in the final half-hour. In fact, for some fans, the defeat has re-contextualised Tuchel’s own tournament: did he bring the right players, and did he deploy his selected group properly?

There were zero minutes for Kobbie Mainoo, while Jordan Henderson’s divisive inclusion gave way to a broken wrist as an unused substitute in a farcical scene. Then there was striker and captain Harry Kane, who scored another healthy helping of goals but is running out of competitions to win gold with England.

Below, we rate every England player at the 2026 World Cup, after the Three Lions signed off with a thrilling 6-4 win over France in the bronze final.

Jordan Pickford – 7/10: No howlers, but not the clean-sheet assuredness we’ve seen in tournaments gone by. In fact, Pickford leaked at least a goal in five of his seven matches and could have done better for DR Congo’s goal and Enzo Fernandez’s equaliser for Argentina. That being said, his performance against Mexico was one of his best, if not his very best, in an England shirt.

Dean Henderson – 5: How about that for a World Cup debut? The Palace stopper was handed his chance in the third-place play-off but conceded four goals to a rampant France in the second-half. In his defence, every goal was a sumptuous finish.

James Trafford – N/A: Didn’t feature at all and, deep down, probably a bit miffed he didn’t get the nod over Henderson on Saturday. However, hard to argue when you’ve been a No 2 all season. He’ll be targeting the No 1 jersey for Euro 2028 but, first, he’ll need to find a No 1 jersey in the Premier League.

Reece James – 6: Such a figure of frustration. When fit and available, his quality is unquestionable, both on the ball with his passing (as shown in his midfield stints) and off the ball with his strong defensive efforts. However, a hamstring issue reared its head and he missed three of England’s eight matches at a crucial time of the tournament.

Jarell Quansah – 3: Tough tournament debut. Trusted at right-back in James’ absence against Mexico, he looked assured until lunging in and receiving a red card. His subsequent two-game ban was a big blow for Tuchel amid a crisis at right-back. And, in hindsight, it was a completely needless lunge-in.

Djed Spence – 7: What a topsy-turvy tournament for the Tottenham defender. Down in the doldrums after a poor display against DR Congo, Spence looked brilliant as a substitute against Mexico and Norway before that starry-eyed display versus Argentina – and that tackle. His pacey recoveries bring back memories of a certain Kyle Walker.

Ezri Konsa – 6: A starter in every match apart from the semi-final against Argentina shows the trust Tuchel placed in the versatile Aston Villa defender. Grew into the tournament after a nervy start against Croatia and deserved his goal against France. Could he have been a bit sharper on an unmarked Lautaro Martinez in stoppage time against Argentina? Perhaps.

John Stones – 6: Looked really rusty against Croatia but, again, grew into the tournament as he played more. A real leader in that England dressing room too, with 94 caps to his name. And we’ll always have that “shoulder injury” dance video….

Trevoh Chalobah: – N/A: Came on in the 93rd minute against France for his only minutes of the tournament. A somewhat odd replacement for wing-back Tino Livramento, it never looked likely that Chalobah would be properly called upon.

Marc Guehi – 7: Oddly dropped for the opener, Guehi then featured from the Ghana game onwards and grabbed the mantle of England’s most accomplished defender. In particular, his nullifying of Man City teammate Erling Haaland set England on their way to the semis.

Dan Burn – 7: A cult figure amongst the fans, Burn’s substitute appearances against Mexico and Norway were iconic, forcefully winning headers and showing a level of passion to make any Englishman proud. And yet, substituting him on against Argentina just gave Lionel Messi and Co even more impetus at the end of the semi-final.

Nico O’Reilly – 6: Dropped for the semi-final, O’Reilly was simply solid throughout. Never looked overawed by any occasion in his first tournament and built up a good understanding with Anthony Gordon on the left. Is it too harsh to say we expected a smidge more going forward given his Man City attacking exploits?

Elliot Anderson – 7: His first tournament with England and a promising start. Was not perfect, but his energy was something to admire, and there were touches of technical quality that were overlooked by many observers. A partnership with Rice would be trusty at future competitions, but is a different profile of midfielder needed (too?).

Declan Rice – 7: Struggled for full fitness as the tournament wore on, preventing him from being at his best, when England really needed him to be. However, he keeps a pretty strong rating here for his right-back stint against DR Congo and that impressive opening goal versus France.

Jordan Henderson – 2: Had the briefest cameo in a nothing spell of the game against Panama, then broke his wrist as an unused substitute against Mexico. Yes, all sympathy to him for that, and without going behind closed doors, we can’t speak to the value of his experience and leadership... but his place this summer was ultimately wasted on him.

Kobbie Mainoo – N/A: Didn’t play a single minute, raising questions over why he was brought at all. Bronze final versus France seemed a surefire moment for him to feature... only for a back injury to rule him out. Could his ability to hold the ball have helped against Argentina? We’ll never know.

Jude Bellingham – 9: Stepped up on so many occasions, showing once again that he is the future of this team – and already more important than Kane, whose six goals he eclipsed with an all-time, English record of seven at a major tournament. His efforts were ceaseless, although that petty streak needs watching...

Morgan Rogers – 6: Showed his quality with that assist for Gordon in the semi-final, which – in another world – could have taken on huge historical importance for England. His initiative to shoot from range against Norway also led to a crucial Bellingham rebound, but these moments were the extent of his contributions, as the ‘Rogers v Bellingham’ No 10 battle never got going.

Bukayo Saka – 7: Boosted his rating with that hat-trick in the thrilling 6-4 win over France, having started the tournament well with three assists to his name. However, in the heart of England’s run, he failed to make his usual mark. That was clearly down to injury struggles, though, which at times begged the question of whether he should have even been brought to the World Cup.

Eberechi Eze – 5: Did well against France after getting his first start of the tournament. Before that, he did not impress as a substitute, but also wasn’t given too many chances to, given Gordon grew into the competition out left and Bellingham was exceptional through the middle.

Noni Madueke – 5: There’s a temptation to go lower here, with the Arsenal winger having been actively poor against Norway, a performance that still sticks out. His tournament peaked in the opener, as he won a spot-kick versus Croatia. However, he did work hard all summer, having been tasked with more minutes than one would’ve expected, due to Saka’s injury issues.

Harry Kane – 7: Given the captain notched another six World Cup goals, plus a crucial assist against Mexico, this rating may feel harsh. After all, he was the hero with a double against DR Congo, crucial with a brace against Croatia, and his penalty versus Mexico also proved essential. But Kane could not make an impact in the quarter-final versus Norway or the semi-final versus Argentina, and those misfires in the biggest moments cost him slightly here.

Ivan Toney – 3: A reflection of his lack of game time, not a lack of quality per se. He was barely given a few minutes at the end of the Argentina tie, and although he started against France, he was unable to get in on the many, many goals.

Ollie Watkins – 3: Again, a rating based on a lack of game time. Had a handful of minutes prior to the second half against France in the bronze final. Surely should have been given more opportunities to show his worth.

Marcus Rashford – 5: That clinical finish against Croatia, in England’s opener, promised so much. Rashford was a revelation off the bench in that game, but he failed to replicate that showing against Ghana, and both his minutes and impact dwindled as the tournament went on.

Anthony Gordon – 7: Deserves immense credit for turning his tournament around, having produced a couple of early performances that rightfully cost him his place in the starting XI. He won a penalty against Mexico and improved further to score against Argentina, a moment he should cherish as much as possible.

title: This is Spain's World Cup final, no matter who wins. Its DNA runs through both teams.
description: From Lionel Messi to Lamine Yamal, Barcelona and Spanish soccer helped shape the stars, styles and ideas that define both finalists.
fullText:
NEW YORK — It is an unthinkable image, impossible to imagine. Lionel Messi scores the winning goal of the World Cup final, running off and kissing the badge of Spain as his teammates surround him in a sea of red shirts.

Unthinkable, but not impossible. After the Rosario native got his start at tiny youth club Abanderado Grandoli and later joined the setup of Newell's Old Boys, he soon moved to FC Barcelona, eventually acquiring Spanish citizenship and becoming eligible for selection by La Roja.

How close was it to happening? Accounts vary. "We tried to get him to play with us ... but he never wanted to," said Ginés Meléndez, then director of Spain's youth national teams, during an interview with Spanish sports news publication "AS" this week.

What is certain is the Argentine federation scrambled together a U-20 friendly match so Messi would know he was wanted and ultimately put on the blue-and-white shirt at the top level.

But while Messi is as Argentine as asado, it's fair to say his soccer DNA is largely Spanish.

Barcelona helped pay for his growth hormone therapy, but also invested time and coaching into the 13-year-old who arrived in Catalonia before he made his pro debut there at 17 and not leaving until he was 34.

In fact, the DNA of top-level Spanish soccer, especially of FC Barcelona and its famed La Masia academy, courses through the World Cup final.

Most national teams are made up of players who have excelled in that country's domestic soccer. While eight members of Luis de la Fuente's Spain squad at this tournament now play outside powerful La Liga, all came through Spanish teams' academies.

Eight of them went through Barcelona, at the same club where Messi went through his development. The most notable is Lamine Yamal, the 19-year-old star who already helped Spain to the 2024 Euro title and now looks to add the World Cup. He may be the closest to a Messi-esque player Barcelona has been able to produce, even with De la Fuente saying Friday that Messi is an "unrepeatable" player.

Yamal is a crafty, left-footed attacker who burst onto the scene at an even younger age than Messi. Though clearly a different player than Messi, who was able to control the ball almost no matter the circumstances and often went on lengthy runs, Yamal's ability to both score and assist — plus his skill shown at a young age — may make him the best Barca player developed at the club since Messi.

Winning a World Cup would only add to his long list of achievements before the age of 20.

But it isn't just Yamal where Barcelona's influence will be felt. Pau Cubarsí is also 19 and has helped Barca to multiple league titles. A center back, his influence isn't as flashy but the work he puts in next to Aymeric Laporte was critical for Spain in the semifinal win over France as it kept a clean sheet for the sixth time this tournament.

Gavi, the untiring midfielder hailed as successor to Xavi, started at Real Betis but moved to La Masia before he made his professional debut. Dani Olmo, Marc Cucurella and Eric García are among the others who developed with Barcelona.

Argentina, too, has deeper roots in Spain than simply Messi's La Masia upbringing. Six players on its squad currently play in Spain. Veterans Rodrigo De Paul and Nicolás Tagliafico, for example, got their first taste of soccer outside Argentina with moves to Spain.

The DNA in this game speaks to Spain's culture of soccer development, of small teams in all corners of a country made up of very distinct climates and even languages putting an emphasis on coaching education, on developing great players and then sending them to larger clubs in the region for further development.

That development doesn't stop at the top level. Perhaps because of the dominance of Barcelona and Real Madrid, almost every other team in the country must think about how it can make the money needed to keep going at the top level. Most have a plan that involves selling top prospects to Barca, Madrid, clubs in the Premier League or another league abroad.

While it was a Spanish club that developed Messi, Argentines also have formed part of Spain's soccer history. Beyond Messi, Diego Maradona, Alfredo Di Stefano and Javier Mascherano, who left their marks on La Liga, the most influential Argentine has been Diego Simeone.

The former Atlético Madrid midfielder has now managed the club since 2011, leading it into a golden era with a distinct style of soccer that borrows from the "potrero," the dirt fields around the country where many Argentines first play. Disciplined but physical, Simeone sets up his team the way he thinks will best disrupt the opponent and give his side the best chance to win.

Now, one of Simeone's sons — 23-year-old Giuliano Simeone — is on the Argentina national team, helping them past England in the semifinal and hoping for another start in the final.

Yet, Spain also has been influenced by Simeone. Creative Villarreal product Álex Baena elevated his play after moving to Simeone at Atletico Madrid, and Marcos Llorente is a fixture for Simeone as one of the poster boys for how the team plays.

Lionel Scaloni, Argentina's manager, is another influenced by Spanish soccer, playing 200 matches with Deportivo La Coruña, while also seeing his ideas and players influence Spanish club play.

"I have an extraordinary admiration for a national team that was Copa América champion, World Cup, Copa América champion again, won the Finalissima, now a World Cup finalist," De la Fuente said Friday. "I don't think anyone has done that in history. And managed by a friend of mine. It’s admiration, admiration and more admiration, and recognition. They're great players."

This article originally appeared on USA TODAY: This is Spain's World Cup final, no matter who wins. Its DNA runs through both teams.

title: Fanatics Debuts “We’re Still Wearing It” Campaign Honoring Lionel Messi Ahead of World Cup Final
description: In what may be the Argentine legend's final game for his nation's national team, the sports company is debuting a film that declares “You Can’t Hang It Up While We’re Still Wearing It.”
fullText:
Hours before Lionel Messi plays in the 2026 FIFA World Cup Final, in what may be his final game on the Argentinian national team, the sports apparel, collectibles and entertainment company Fanatics is launching a campaign that focuses on Messi’s iconic number 10 jersey and declares “You Can’t Hang It Up While We’re Still Wearing It.”

Fanatics debuted a short film called “We’re Still Wearing It” Sunday morning, which features a Spanish-language version of the song “Don’t Cry For Me Argentina” by Luz Gaggi, a finalist on Argentina’s The Voice.

More from The Hollywood Reporter

Where to Watch France vs. England World Cup Third-Place Playoff Online

Disney's Next Big Product Line Sees Its Star Characters Collide With NFL Teams

The NBA Wants to Set Next Season's TV Schedule, But It's Waiting On LeBron

The film sees a giant Messi jersey being raised in his honor, a metaphor for his presumed retirement, before the citizens rally to tear it down and express their desire to see him continue to play.

Fanatics says that the film, which is set in Buenos Aires, was cast entirely with real fans of Messi, going so far as to source extras from an Argentinian fan group. It is also filled with Easter eggs that reference Messi and his career, including jerseys that span his entire run for the Argentinian national team.

Over the next two days, Fanatics will also erect “We’re Still Wearing It” billboards in a dozen cities from around the world, many with connections to Messi, including in Rosario, Argentina, where Messi was born; Barcelona and Paris, cities where he’s played; Lake Town, India, home to a 70-foot Messi statue. Other cities include New York, Buenos Aires, London, Mexico City, Los Angeles, Lagos, Tokyo and São Paulo.

Later today, Messi and Argentina will play in the World Cup final in New Jersey, facing off against Spain. While Messi will return to the MLS team Inter Miami after the World Cup is over, his future with the Argentinian national team is in doubt at 39 years old.

title: Spain or Argentina, who will win the FIFA World Cup? Ex-player predictions
description: Former player predictions as the European and South American champions clash in a World Cup final for the first time.
fullText:
The FIFA World Cup 2026 final is not far away, and the predictions are coming in thick and fast.

Spain, the reigning European champions, are eyeing their second World Cup title, 16 years on from their maiden triumph in South Africa. But standing in their way are holders Argentina, the South American giants aiming to become just the third country to win back-to-back World Cup titles.

Stats provider Opta predicts Spain as the favourites to win the 2026 World Cup, with a 59.56 percent probability of lifting the trophy in New Jersey. La Roja are also the picks of several former players and pundits.

Former England striker Michael Owen said world number two Spain are the “deserved” winners of the tournament.

“Spain haven’t got the greatest individual players, I think France probably have … but Spain as a team are everything England are not – they are brave, courageous with the ball,” Owen told The Straits Times.

“Spain are just a great team that pass the ball confidently and play amazing football as a collective.”

Zlatan Ibrahimovic, the ex-Barcelona and Manchester United striker who represented Sweden at two World Cups, expects Spain to dominate the final, which comes two years on from their Euro 2024 triumph.

Spain handled France very good [in the semifinals]. I believe they can handle this Argentina,” Zlatan, now a pundit on US broadcaster Fox Sports, said.

“Argentina is not a counterattacking team. Spain are going to do their thing. They’re going to play collectively … I believe Spain are going to dominate that game.”

Three lions fell just short but nothing but pride for this country…

Going 2-1 Spain tomorrow! 
Lamine Yamal ⚽️⚽️ https://t.co/CBw7Gxykiw

— Rio Ferdinand (@rioferdy5) July 18, 2026

Spain were also John Terry’s favourites for glory, with the former England captain saying on the Sports Uncensored show that Luis de la Fuente’s side have “got too much for Argentina”.

Former goalkeeper Iker Casillas, the Spain captain during their maiden World Cup triumph in 2010, expressed his confidence in the team’s ability to bring home a second, sharing a picture of another star halfway stitched on the top of their badge.

A star represents every World Cup a team has won.

Y por qué no? pic.twitter.com/IB9wTlcGYD

— Iker Casillas (@IkerCasillas) July 16, 2026

While a lot of the consensus centred around a Spain win, some think Argentina have a chance, even if it is slim.

Alan Shearer, one of England’s greatest strikers of all time, backed the reigning champions, saying on The Rest is Football podcast, “If there’s one team that can do it in terms of stopping them [Spain], it’s Argentina because of their mentality and togetherness.

“They have been down so many times and in trouble, and somehow managed to get a win. That’s what you’ve got to admire about them.

“When you’ve got a genius in your side,” he added, referring to Messi, “you’ve always got a chance.”

But how can Argentina beat favourites Spain in the title clash?

English football pundit Micah Richards thinks the answer to that is low-block transition – the strategy of teams sitting back closest to their goal and, after winning possession, rapidly shifting from a compact defensive shape into an offensive counterattack

“Get the ball to Messi, whereas [Julian] Alvarez or [Lisandro] Martinez just make it horrible, make it how they did with England,” he added.

“I think Argentina need to stay in the game as they did against England and turn it on when they see a shift in momentum.”

Meanwhile, another of England’s greats, Gary Lineker, stuck with his pretournament pick, Spain, but added that Argentina are “so gritty”, making the predictions a very tough choice.

“You cannot go against that little fella [Messi]. He’s so great; it’s going to be really, really close. It’s genuinely 50-50.”

Unlike others, New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani refused to pick a side.

“Everyone I root for has lost,” he said, with a laugh. “So I feel like whoever I say is going to lose at this point.

“I’m gonna say the classic politician’s answer – I’m excited for a good game.”

title: What Scottie Scheffler told Ted Scott about his ball-striking which left him shocked when they started working together
description: Ted Scott revealed that Scottie Scheffler claimed that his ball-striking was a weakness when they first began to work together, as he reflected on being on the bag for the world number one. It has been a truly remarkable few years for Scheffler.
fullText:
Ted Scott revealed that Scottie Scheffler claimed that his ball-striking was a weakness when they first began to work together, as he reflected on being on the bag for the world number one.

It has been a truly remarkable few years for Scheffler. The 30-year-old went into the 2022 season on the PGA Tour still awaiting a first victory.

As the 2026 major championship season draws to a close, Scheffler can now reflect on four major victories, with 16 further wins coming on the PGA Tour.

Ted Scott has been his caddie for the large majority of that time. The pair began working together shortly after Scheffler made his Ryder Cup debut back in 2021.

Incredibly, it seems that one person who did not see such success just around the corner was Scottie Scheffler himself.

Speaking in a past Bible Caddie podcast, Scott explained what Scheffler identified as a problem within his game when their partnership began.

“It’s really amazing especially because when I first started working for him, he told me, he said, ‘I’m not a very good ball-striker’. And after week one, I was like, ‘buddy, are you sure you’re not a very good ball-striker? Who’s telling you this? What’s wrong with you? You hit everything directly at the tree I’m pointing at, driver included’,” he said.

“It’s interesting how we perceive ourselves. He’s a very confident man, he has a belief in himself, and he’s a really hard worker, super hard worker. Humble, he’ll ask for help, he’s not afraid to tell his team, ‘look, I’m struggling with this, can you help me figure this out?’ He’s just got a really cool heart to be great, and it’s been amazing to stand [next to him].”

In fairness to Scheffler, he had good reason to think that his ball-striking could be better.

He was actually 83rd on tour for approach play during the 2021 season. It is easy to forget now that he was a slightly contentious pick for the Ryder Cup at Whistling Straits.

The 2026 season is likely to be his worst season for strokes gained approach since then.

However, he was fourth in 2022, before finishing first in that category for three seasons in a row. So it is no wonder that Scott was left completely baffled by Scheffler’s criticism of himself.

Scheffler’s challenge for 2027 will be to return to that sort of level after a year which does look set to be a disappointment by his incredible standards.
